Name: Brock Bowers
Outlook: Bowers was a revelation as a rookie, setting the all-time rookie TE record for receptions in a single season (112) while finishing as the TE1 overall. Sure, Jakobi Meyers and Jack Bech are fine talents, but there’s no question that Bowers is the top dog in the pass game, he got a huge upgrade at QB with Geno Smith now in town, and this man is due for more TDs after he saw just six (!!) total targets inside the 10-yard line. You’ll have to pay up in drafts to get him, but honestly, we can’t fault anyone for trying to get a stud like Bowers at fantasy’s most frustrating position.

Name: Trey McBride
Outlook: T McB! If you want a TE who could outscore Brock Bowers as fantasy’s TE1, this is your dude. McBride was dominant last year with 111 catches and over 1,100 yards. He was fantasy’s TE3 in half PPR formats despite scoring just twice! If he can replicate those reception and yardage totals and catch the right side of TD variance, McBride is fully capable of having one of those peak Gronk or Kelce-type seasons.

Name: George Kittle
Outlook: Without Brandon Aiyuk last year and CMC missing most of the season, Kittle was an absolute monster, posting his highest yardage total since 2018 as fantasy’s TE2 behind only Brock Bowers. With Deebo Samuel now in Washington, Aiyuk recovering from a torn ACL, and Ricky Pearsall a bit of an unknown entering Year 2, Kittle should once again push for the team lead in targets. He's a locked in weekly top-5 play at the position, and if you don’t want to spend up for Bowers or McBride in drafts, Kittle is a great “consolation” prize in the middle rounds.

Name: T.J. Hockenson
Outlook: Hockenson didn’t get on the field until Week 9 last season as he spent the first half of the year recovering from a torn ACL. When he came back, we saw more of what we’ve become accustomed to for Hockenson - some nice volume games but moments where he disappears. Playing next to Justin Jefferson and Jordan Addison, that’s just inevitable in this offense. His ultimate ceiling in fantasy this year will be tied to how well JJ McCarthy can play in his first season as a starter. We expect him to be a top-12 guy at the position, but as long as Jefferson and Addison are healthy, the ceiling is capped for fantasy.

Name: Sam LaPorta
Outlook: We had LaPorta as a fade last year, not because we don’t believe in the talent, but because his ADP was priced in a way where he almost had to repeat as the TE1 overall. In other words, there wasn’t much room for error. Repeating as the TE1 in back-to-back seasons is a very tough thing to do, and we saw that play out last year as LaPorta “regressed” to the TE7 in 2024. Now that his ADP is more appropriate, he is a fine target if you are hoping to draft a plug-and-play guy. Just be aware that there’s a lot of mouths to feed in this Detroit offense, so you may have to ride it out with the ups and downs if Amon-Ra, Jamo, or the RBs are stealing the show that week.

Name: Travis Kelce
Outlook: It’s kinda funny how dominant Kelce has been. His TE6 finish a year ago was considered a “down year” for the future Hall of Famer. He has now finished as the TE8 or better in EVERY YEAR AS A STARTER. Folks, that is incredible. Gone are the days of Kelce being the TE1 and only real difference maker at the position that can lap the field, but that doesn’t mean he won’t have value as a plug-and-play option, even at the ripe young age of 35. His yardage totals have dropped in three straight years, but he is a sneaky bounce-back candidate in the TD department. He is coming off a career low in TD catches despite seeing 133 targets a season ago. If that TD total bounces back to the 7-10 range, he can still flirt with TE3-TE5 production. Don’t sleep on the old man.

Name: David Njoku
Outlook: It hasn’t always been consistent, but over the last three years, Njoku has averaged five catches and about 50 yards per game. Those numbers aren’t going to win you your league, but they are good enough to keep “The Chief” on the radar as a low-end TE1. Njoku’s athleticism after the catch is awesome, but the QB room is anything but...there’s a real chance Njoku is catching passes from rookies Shedeur Sanders and/or Dillon Gabriel at the end of the season, so don’t go into your drafts thinking you have a locked in starter for the entire year. That said, if it’s Joe Flacco under center, it’s pants off szn with the hope that this duo can recreate their magical run at the end of 2023.

Name: Evan Engram
Outlook: Engram regressed in a big way in 2024 after he caught 114 passes in 2023. Of course, he missed a month and a half with a hamstring injury, which certainly didn’t help, but we saw his receptions per game drop from 6.7 to 5.8. For a player who’s always been pretty volume dependent with a low average depth of target, any drop in volume is going to have a pretty big impact on his fantasy value. Fortunately for Easy E, he gets a fresh start in Sean Payton’s offense with a depth chart that doesn’t really have a locked in #2 option behind Courtland Sutton. There is a chance Engram can be that “Joker” in Payton’s offense and be awesome for fantasy, but don’t be surprised when it’s Week 5, and they are dialing up play action looks for Nate Adkins or Adam Trautman. We know Payton loves to rotate his guys…

Name: Tucker Kraft
Outlook: Kraft ended up being a complete steal late in drafts a year ago, and in fact, he was probably undrafted in most normal 10 or 12-man leagues. If you were able to add him for essentially free, he paid off in a big way, finishing as the TE9 on the back of 700 yards and seven scores. Entering 2025, we feel good about Kraft’s opportunity as the lead guy in the TE room for Green Bay, but just be mindful that he probably has a wider range of outcomes than his ADP might suggest. He was a YAC machine last year, averaging 8.8 YAC per reception. That mark was a full two yards higher than the next qualified TE. If that regresses in any way, Kraft could disappoint in the yardage department. He only averaged 2.9 receptions per game, so like most TEs going in this range of the draft, he is probably going to be TD-or-bust most weeks. Fortunately, the Packers offense should score in bunches this year.

Name: Mark Andrews
Outlook: Well, Marky Mark. We do love ya, but let’s acknowledge one thing from 2024. You, sir, were one lucky son of a gun! Andrews scored 11 (!!) TDs last year despite catching just 3.2 passes per game. He and McBride were basically the complete opposite of each other, with Andrews perhaps the most TD-reliant player in the game. His targets, receptions, yards, and routes per game have all come down in three straight seasons. The warning signs are there…buyer beware on a guy who is due for major regression.

Name: Jake Ferguson
Outlook: Turd Ferguson, whose nickname is just simply the best, was a bit of a fantasy turd himself last year. Fergy was one of those guys who was drafted as a Top-10 TE in 2024 after a really nice 2023 season, but the bottom fell out in this Cowboys pass game after Dak’s injury, and Ferguson ran ice cold in the TD department. The government doesn’t want you to remember that Ferguson was on pace for over 800 yards prior to Dak’s season-ending hamstring injury. If you’re punting the position, he’s a sneaky later round target this year with Dak back under center and the TD luck potentially swinging back in his direction after he found the end zone the same number of times as Owl Borland a year ago.

Name: Mike Gesicki
Outlook: “Sticky” as he’s known around Ballers HQ, popped up for some big games as part of the Bengals’ high-flying aerial attack last season. Just remember, those big games primarily came with Tee Higgins out of the lineup. Without Tee, Sticky G averaged nine targets per game, but with Higgins active, he only saw 5.6 targets per game. For the most part, Gesicki is on the streaming radar as Joe Burrow’s “slot WR.” There are probably better late round dart throws at TE, but if Ja’Marr Chase or Tee Higgins miss time this season, expect Gesicki to be the hotness on the waiver wire.

Name: Dallas Goedert
Outlook: Goedert’s 2025 season outlook looked a bit murky there entering the NFL Draft, as he was involved in potential trade rumors. After restructuring his deal, he’s back in Philly, which is a bit of a double-edged sword. On one hand, he’s attached to one of the best offenses in football. On the other hand, Saquon Barkley, A.J. Brown, and DeVonta Smith are the alphas in this offense, and we know Jalen Hurts is going to score 10+ TDs with his legs thanks to the tush push. It’s hard to trust him in your lineup every week, but we know that if one of Smith or Brown misses time, Goedert sees a big boost in target share.

Name: Colston Loveland
Outlook: It’s crowded in Chicago…DJ Moore, Rome Odunze, Luther Burden, and of course, Loveland are all exciting pieces in Ben Johnson’s offense. The fact that this situation is really tough to predict creates some uncertainty around the rookie’s Year 1 role. That uncertainty could present a value in drafts if Loveland can emerge as a legit weapon in this offense. We’re excited about most of the options tied to Johnson and Caleb, but this situation has a wide range of outcomes. We don’t mind taking shots later in drafts on the rookie, but be ready to pivot if the WRs dominate the targets early on.
